ministry_positions.notes: Ordained November 13, 1761. "Resided at Greenfield, where he was pastor of church for 53 years" per EH (actual span closer to 55 years, 1761-1816).

cross_connections.display_label: She left him a £30 Yale bequest he fulfilled in 1758

narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1758 and was ordained at the First Church in Greenfield, Massachusetts on November 18, 1761. He served Greenfield for fifty-six years until his death in December 1816.

narrative_blocks.header: Aunt's £30 bequest set the ministry in motion
narrative_blocks.body: His aunt Alice Newton Buckingham left him £30 for college if he proved 'likely for public service' — he was barely three years old when she made the bequest. He graduated Yale eighteen years later and fulfilled her hope.

narrative_blocks.header: Town stayed a single parish all 56 years
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted Greenfield's call on the condition that all persons of 'competent knowledge and sober lives' be permitted communion, avoiding doctrinal narrowness. The entire town remained united in a single parish throughout his ministry — an unusual achievement in the fractious colonial church landscape.

narrative_blocks.header: Baptized an enslaved girl called Tinner in 1767
narrative_blocks.body: In his record of baptisms for September 20, 1767, he noted 'Tinner, my negro girl' — an enslaved person in his household. This stands in tension with his liberal communion policy and the harmony of his ministry.
On May 23, 1812 he wrote in his diary: 'I shall not and I would not live always.' He died four years later in December 1816, in his eightieth year, having outlived his wife by eleven years.

parents.mother_research_notes: Sarah Welch Fowler, second wife of Samuel Newton

marriage_date.notes: Married early 1724; deed dated June 18, 1724 gives "Abner Newton of Milford and Mary his wife"

primary_residence.notes: Removed from Milford to Durham, settling there in late 1724. Deed of July 1724 calls him "Abner Newton of Milford"; deed of December 1724 calls him "Abner Newton, late of Milford, now of Durham."

narrative_blocks.header: Half-brother shared same first name, Abner
narrative_blocks.body: This Abner Newton was born 1699 to Samuel Newton's second wife Sarah Welch Fowler — distinguishing him from his half-brother Abner Newton baptized May 1689, who had been born to Samuel's first wife Martha Fenn ten years earlier. The repeated Abner name across both marriages was uncommon. The 1699 Abner became the documented ancestor of the Durham Newton branch — earning the 'durham_newton' tag in this entry.

narrative_blocks.header: 1724 Milford-to-Durham move; wife sold inherited share
narrative_blocks.body: In 1724 Abner Newton and Mary Burwell removed from Milford to Durham Connecticut — and on the move Mary sold her own inherited property, a quarter share of a dwelling house and home-lot in Milford, to Susannah Burwell (widow of John Burwell) for eighty pounds. Per the deed boundaries this was the homestead of the first John Burwell, occupied since 1639. Mary had kept a share in her family's original Milford homestead until the day she left for Durham.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Roger received Alice Newton's Yale bequest
narrative_blocks.body: Abner's son Roger Newton was the beneficiary of Alice Newton's ca.1740 conditional Yale bequest — establishing the family's Yale-funded ministerial track. Roger graduated Yale 1758 and served fifty-six years as minister at Greenfield Massachusetts until 1816 — one of the longest continuous American pastorates of the Revolutionary-Republican era.

INST068.name: Brown University
INST068.notes:
Co-founded 1764 as the College of Rhode Island. Ezra Stiles (HD0346) was among its founders — not the sole founder. Renamed Brown University 1804.
